text: The surgeon performs diagnostic laparoscopy, using laparoscopic instruments to reduce the omentum, which is visible as the content of the incisional hernia.
The surgeon uses a monopolar current and a blunt grasper to manipulate the hernia sac, which is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a scale to measure the size of the hernia defect, preparing to place an appropriately sized mesh at the beginning of the procedure. A barb suture and mesh are visible as part of the initial setup.
The robotic arms are docked, and the surgeon begins the dissection process on the incisional hernia site.

The surgeon begins with a transabdominal retromuscular approach, moving inferiorly where the scar tissue is encountered. The peritoneum appears flimsy and thin, leading to buttonholing or division. The distal part of the sac is either addressed robotically or through a small incision to remove it entirely.
The surgeon divides the sac completely, creating a visible hole in the peritoneal aspect where the sac was previously connected, and continues to dissect further down.
The surgeon creates a surgical plane that becomes the space of Retzius in the midline and the space of Bogros laterally on either side.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the inferior epigastric vessels on the sides. As the dissection progresses lower, attention is given to avoid the glomerular bladder and retro pubic veins.

The surgeon performs a gentle dissection, following the loose areolar tissue to maintain the correct surgical plane. Small vessels encountered are cauterized to achieve hemostasis, ensuring a clear field as the dissection progresses to create a large space.

The surgeon measures the defect, which is approximately four by five centimeters, and uses a tailored mesh measuring thirteen by fifteen centimeters to ensure adequate overlap and complete coverage of the space.

The surgeon creates a well-defined and tailored space for the mesh placement, ensuring it sits comfortably without creases. The mesh is positioned without extensive fixation to minimize postoperative pain and allow for better integration.
The surgeon achieves hemostasis in the surgical field, focusing on the right side where the dissection plane is slightly elevated.

The surgeon navigates the surgical field using robotic instruments, carefully re-entering the correct anatomical plane to ensure the inferior epigastric vessels are preserved on the roof of the dissection area.
The surgeon divides small blood vessels and encounters a small lump of fat, identified as preperitoneal fat herniation, across a small defect likely due to a previous cesarean section.
The surgeon navigates from the space of Phrygius to the space of Bogros, creating an adequately sized dissection area of a couple of centimeters without extending it unnecessarily.

The surgeon assesses the groin hernia, ensuring adequate space has been created and hemostasis achieved. The mesh is positioned satisfactorily without creases, and the surgeon prepares to close the hernia defect.
The surgeon makes final adjustments in the area where the mesh will be placed, ensuring no tissue remains that could cause the mesh to tent.

The surgeon creates a smooth plane in the retropubic area, ensuring the mesh does not fold. The anatomical landmarks are clearly visible as the surgeon uses a backhand technique with a barbed suture for ergonomic suturing.
The surgeon closes the hernia defect, ensuring a snug and strong closure without strangulation, while preventing the mesh from protruding into the defect.
The surgeon completes the closure of the hernia defect, ensuring a snug and secure repair. The surgical field is prepared for mesh placement, with the closure appearing visually satisfactory.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 Vicryl suture to take a small bite at the midline of the mesh, aligning it with the body's midline for symmetrical placement. The mesh is then fixed just behind the pubic arch.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection in an area with no sizeable vessels or major nerve supply, ensuring safe fixation of the mesh. A stitch is placed to secure the mesh, minimizing its movement, before proceeding with the closure of the PRS Peritoneum Complex.
The surgeon uses a III-O Stratafix barbed suture to close the defect, taking two to three consecutive bites before pulling to achieve optimal alignment.

The surgeon completes the peritoneal closure, ensuring the sutures cross the midline and extend to the opposite side. The closure appears aesthetically pleasing, with attention given to small buttonholes and the herniated defect.

The surgeon addresses small defects in the peritoneum, opting to close the main incision first to avoid complications. Defects larger than five to eight millimeters are closed using figure-of-eight or simple sutures to prevent bowel or omentum from entering and causing postoperative complications.
